deployment_question: How does the model perform when train and test are sampled from the same observed mixture?
definition: Randomly assign examples to train, validation, and test without explicitly controlling scientific similarity, group membership, time, site, or domain. In SPECTRA this is a baseline operating point that often lies near the high-overlap end of the curve.

split_operator.spectra_mapping: sample a high-overlap reference point before constructing harder novelty points

implementation_recipe: Shuffle samples with a recorded seed.
Assign samples according to the requested proportions or folds.
After splitting, measure train-test similarity and group overlap rather than assuming IID behavior.
quality_gates: Report the random seed and split proportions.
Measure duplicate, near-duplicate, and group leakage with a domain similarity axis.
Do not use this as the only generalization claim when deployment is non-IID.
failure_modes: Near-duplicates or related samples can occur in both train and test.
Performance can be optimistic when deployment requires novel structures, sites, times, or groups.
Class balance can hide domain imbalance.

citations.title: UMAP-based clustering split for rigorous evaluation of AI models for virtual screening
citations.evidence: Reports that random splits are easier than scaffold, Butina, or UMAP-based molecular splits.

citations.title: GraphPart: homology partitioning for biological sequence analysis
citations.evidence: Motivates homology-aware partitioning because random splits can place related biological sequences across train and test.
